sentences.text: My name is Ivan Devai, D-E-V-A-I.
Yes.
Ervin.
E-R-V-I-N.
Yes.
My name was originally Deutsch, D-E-U-T-S-C-H.
I changed it in 1949 in Hungary.
Partly because I had to sort of say get lost.
And partly because it was customary by that time to change German sounding names to something more Hungarians.
They couldn't spell the name.
It gave problem so I decided to change it then.
I was born in 1932, 2nd of April, in Budapest, Hungary.
That makes me 66 years old.

Look, before the war, I was a reasonably young and Budapest for me was that trams and the wide avenues and the snow in winter and-- and the flowers and gardens and that sort of things in-- in summertime.
School, family, going out into the mountains over to Buda with my father, with mother, with their friends and their children.
We lived a very normal, civilized middle-class life.
My father was working hard.
Mother was running the household.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big family get togethers at my grandma's place, at our place.
And that was mostly what happening.
Yes.
Definitely.
Definitely.
I was the first grandchild, so I was a lot with my grandparents, who adored me.
By that time, they were retired so they had [BACKGROUND NOISE] really time to spend with me, to teach me how to ride the bike, and all that sort of things.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Very close.
Very close.
We were very close knit family.
And very close.
My grandfather name was Appel, Appel Fulop, [BACKGROUND NOISE] with the P in the middle what was standing for [?
Pincus. ?] He came from a little village up [BACKGROUND NOISE] parts of Hungary.
And there the Jewish boys had only the Hebrew name.
He only took on Fulop [BACKGROUND NOISE] when he came up to Budapest.
Grandmother's name is Roza [?
Stenfeld. ?]
That's my mother's.
[SIGHS] The grandparents on my father's side, I did not know.
We were mostly more close to my mother's side.
My mother had two sisters and a brother.
One of the sisters was married.
The other sister was not.
She was a kindergarten teacher.
She had her own kindergarten.
I went to that kindergarten.
Later my sister went to that kindergarten.
And also my mother's brother, Arpad, who we, too, we were very closely attached.
My father's sister lived next door to us with two sons.
Her name was Semmerlei, S-E-- double-M-- E-R-L-E-I, [?
Bertie ?] and [?
Gizzie ?] and [BACKGROUND NOISE] the children's name was [?
Lotsi ?] and [?
Pichta. ?] Good Hungarian names all around.
[SIGHS]
My mother's sister name is Klein [BACKGROUND NOISE] Beszi, Erzsebet-- Erzsebet and the younger one is Magda.
Oh, we children usually played out in the backyard, we had-- we had back-- big backyard.
And older ones we were sitting inside talking politics and family stories and this and that.
It's not a-- not a quite clear picture.
Grandma came over and did some special cooking.
And the family was together.
I think that was the-- the main point of it, not what we're doing.
It was just to be together.
I think so.
I think so.
Yes, I have a sister.
My sister name is Marica.
No.
I-- it was only the two of us.
And then [?
Bergie's ?] son Peter, who was nearly my sister's age.
He was part of the-- he was like a brother to us.
We grown up together.
My sister is four years younger than I am.
In my opinion, yes.
She said that I was bossing her too much.
She still tells me that.
It's not a garden.
It's just a backyard.
Our-- we had our own house.
And we had the factory in the back.
And originally it was a dairy product factory.
And the-- the horse driven carts came into the house.
And we had a big backyard where they were able to turn around and get loaded and went off to deliver the milk products.
And that was there.
My father owned the business.
In our way, yes.

Because-- because that was the business was there and we lived there and it was a business originally where you have to start very early morning when the milk start to come in and then get it produced, pasteurized, and made the milk into-- into cheese and butter or whatever.
That was very, very early [LAUGHS] time.
I only know about it from the stories.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Only later when that factory, the license was taken away, then my father started to repair and make machinery and milk cans for the industry.
I don't know exactly, something has to do with Jewish laws and problems with the suppliers who were not very happy to supply Jews.
I don't-- I haven't got a clear answer on that.
That was too early in my time.
He was able.
My father was a very able man and a very hard working man.
And he had his connection.
And he was very well-liked in the trade.
And that's why he was able to start working with them.
My father was a gentleman, a Hungarian gentleman in appearance, in-- in way of behaving.
He had a high school.
He had beautiful writing.
He was a good businessman.
Apparently, even from POW camp, he came back with things what he got together in-- in Siberia, his way home, home, through China.
He was dealing in gold and-- and brought back gold coins and the rings.
And he was spotting an artisan there and he made up gold rings and-- and sold them and that sort of things.
First World War, yes.
I got on very well with my father.
I was a very young boy.
I looked up to him very much.
But he was working very hard.
We did not spend so much time together because he was working.
It was more Mom's business to-- to look after the kids and to keep us entertained and take us to school or to-- to other children's birthday parties and that sort of things.
Father was the figure who arrived for dinner and had to have his peace and read his newspaper and-- and read-- and drink his tea in the Russian way with the half sugar between his teeth.
This I remember.
[LAUGHS]
He was a Hungarian gentleman.
He came from Heves Megye, what was the most Hungarian and Hungarian area in Hungary.
Definitely.
Definitely.
He was a military man.
He served his time and with distinction.
And he was very Hungarian Hungarian.
But he was Israelite and Shabbat was strictly kept with Friday evening dinner and challah and no light switching and no smoking and no writing and all these things.
He had his first low seat in the synagogue.
And very strictly Israelite.
And he was eating with his hat on.
And make his [INAUDIBLE] and washed his hand, and we have Pesach and Rosh Hashana and all the main holy days with all the things what goes with it.
Definitely.
Definitely.
Look, I didn't-- I didn't-- I couldn't say strict or not strict because that was the only thing I-- I know.
That was what I seen at my grandparents' at my aunties' at my uncles'.
That was the thing.
We didn't eat out, only in a fish restaurant or in a number of-- of kosher restaurants, what was available in Budapest.
And I think that was one of the reason why the family kept together, and we went to each other place because we knew we that we have the right sort of food as it's supposed to be.
Typical Shabbat evening was that dad came in and everybody was washed and dressed and Mom put up the usual Shabbat dinner.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Soup, chicken soup, naturally, and chicken and-- and Shabbat had a special sauce.
What we usually had on the Friday evening.
And there was the two challah.
And that made the Motzi and the Kaddish for the-- for the wine.
And--
Motzi is-- Motzi, cutting of the challah.
And you giving a piece to everybody.
No.
We had the Shabbos [? goyter ?] who did that sort of jobs to switch on and off a light.
He's a-- he's a non-Jewish person who allowed to do that sort of things.
We had a living girl who her mom were running the houseld.
She was doing that sort of things.
Yes.
Yes.
Our-- we-- we lived in-- in Lubowitz [?
Motzo, ?] what is on the Eighth District.
And four or five corners away was the synagogue in [?
Baroshutso. ?] And that's where we always went, actually walking.
That was walking distance easily.
And the synagogue wasn't a-- a full building.
It was in a-- in a flat house.
One floor of the flat house was broken slew and separate men's part and the woman strictly, kept away with a wall.
And we had an old, for me, an old rabbi, Rabbi Stern.
He was also--
He was also my teacher in the elementary school, teaching Jewish things.
And he came to our house many times because also my uncle who lived next door, [?
Bertie ?] was very religious.
And that was our synagogue.
That's where we went.
There was a big yard outside and all the kids, we were just running up and down there.
And when that became very angry, then he grabbed me and made me sit down between him and [?
Bertie ?] [?
Bachi. ?] and that was the High Holy Days.
Baroshutso ?] Synagogue, nothing special.
That was a reasonably large family synagogue, not one of the big built synagogues.
And I remember before Yom Kippur to go out next to us was a big garden like-- like a botanical garden.
And there where we feeding the fishes before Yom Kippur and the [INAUDIBLE], the chicken, with the chickens and taking it to the butcher to get it cut after.
Yes.
Yes.
I think so.
[DRINKS]
Look, my mother was a little lady even then, reasonably well fed.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived about three houses from the corner.
The corner was the bus stop.
In many cases, my mother took a taxi when she went over to Auntie [?
Bergie ?] or went into the city because she just couldn't walk because her feet was hurting and all that.
It's still hurting thanks goodness.
I think so.
We had what we needed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to-- to the Jewish high school.
What was an expensive exercise.
We dressed well.
We eat well.
Some we-- we went on holidays for two, three weeks.
Don't ask me where.
To a lake.
I don't remember.
But we went.
They came with big horse-drawn carriage and took the things away.
And my father once made me a boat about that size to play with it on the lake.
They were happy times I think.
No, it was mother, Marica, me, usually her sister Beszi was coming with Peter too.
And then the husbands just came down for the weekend.
Not really.
It was very in the-- in the-- in the family.
Yes.
No.
Primary school I went just next door was the normal Hungarian elementary school.
I did four years there.
Duganitchuzi ?] Elementary School.
My cousins went there.
There was a lot of Jewish kids there.
At the elementary school, sure.
Sure.
With a number of-- a large number of them.
But also there was a big difference because next door to us was a building full of it-- very high ranking army officers and their children came to that school.
On the other side of the street was the Ludovico Academia, what is a Hungarian officers' school.
And the commander of that school and many of the teachers, high ranking officers, lived just next door to us.
Many of their kids were our friends.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Yes.
At that time, yes.
I don't think my father had any close friends.
He had many friends from-- from the army and from POW times.
Very, rarely he went-- there was a very strong organization [?
Frongtard ?] [?
Shoshevtiz, ?] what is something like the RSL here, the returned soldiers, and my father very proudly wore its emblem.
And he was a member there.
I don't think he went there too many times.
He was working too hard.
Not really.
Because in that school, there was too many Jewish.
Quite a number of Jewish teachers still.
So it was kept on a-- on a very under level.
And we're talking-- we're talking early years, we're talking 1938.
It-- it-- not really.
Look, there was always a couple of big mouths.
But generally, it was frowned upon.
And-- and we were Hungarians and we stood up and we-- we sing the Himnusz.
And the only difference was that we went to a different class when it came to religious education.
Yes.
Yes.
Not.
Not.
Look, we had a school cap naturally.
And it had a big I in it, what meant Israel-- Israelite, RG, Realgymnasium.
And we always said only that this reformatus gymnasium.
We didn't-- we ignored the I on it because we had to wear the hat, and we didn't want to be too much recognized us as Israelite people.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
We just ignored it.
We said can't you see, it's RG, it's reformat gymnasium.
Not that I would recall.
Only group I belonged to then was the-- the scout group.
That was a Jewish scout group from the-- from the school, 297 [INAUDIBLE] USF scout group.
And by that time, Jewish scout group wasn't a lot.
So were behind the cover of our Red Cross group.
But we did the normal scout things.
And instead of the scout symbol, we had a little emblem what just said 297.
I was 10 years old.
Till-- till the school was on-- the school, I think, lost somewhere in '44, March, end of March, the school closed.
Yes.
Oh, we did our touring, we did our learning of scout laws and scout things.
And--
No.
No.
The only thing that we not allowed to wear uniform, scout uniform.
We didn't have scout flag.
That was the difference.
But we kept up the scout-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what the scout groups do.
Yes.
Yes.
It kept us together and kept us occupied.
And gave us a chance to-- to fill in our hearts, whatever we wouldn't have anything to do and keep up the spirits.
And we thought that if we come, then we can join the real scout movement.
Yes.
Few.
Few.
Tommy ?] [?
Shay, ?] [?
Gabby ?] [?
Singer ?] names come-- come to mind.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a loose, quite large group.
And it was basically the same group I've seen in school.
They're very-- I think they were very peaceful, well-balanced, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very normal times.
No.
No.
Not at all.
Not at all.
It wasn't talked about.
It wasn't-- no, no.
Don't forget in-- in 1944, I was only 12 years old.

And for a 12-year-old as long as I had my good food and my chocolate and my books and my friends and mommy and daddy around and grandpa and we can go out on the weekend to [INAUDIBLE] and up in the mountains and have a good time, I don't think we had any special concerns.
Look, father closed down the business somewhere in '34 or '35.
I was eight years old.
By the time I started to-- to know what's happening, we had a factory that was working with six, eight people.
And even saw that he worked very hard.
And my mom, many times, was screaming out to her to the back to [INAUDIBLE], that's enough, come in, finish it.
You know?
Life was-- looked very, very normal.
